title: Matters of Canonicity Split
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Dec 14, 2007 11:33 pm Reichu wrote: "Too bad!"

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Dec 15, 2007 5:18 am Tiers of Canonicity is a critical establishment for the website, Jimbo. You'll have to get over your hangups for the sake of the Greater Good, at least.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 15, 2007 9:05 am By Reichu's command.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sat Dec 15, 2007 9:06 am It shall be as the Webmummy directs it.

[]:             Quote Post by Shin-seiki » Sat Dec 15, 2007 10:10 am I just don't get where people that claim to have reservations about the CI are coming from. To me the most notable thing about it is how very congruently it seems to fit with the backstory elements that turn up in the series and movies (especially as regards the NPC/EoE stuff). I suspect that some people might not care for it because its revelations are inconvenient to their own pet theories and pre-conceived notions, but hey, as Anno would say, "Too bad!" . If you want to impeach the canonicity of the CI, then point out instances where it's inconsistent with the First Tier Canon; as far as I know there are none, but even if you could produce such an example, the way the Tiers work is that you throw out that one questionable statement, not the whole thing. The idea that a single mistake nullifies the credibility of an entire outside source was favored by the infamous Super-Troll FuzzyChickens (with regard to the RCB), which is indicative of the virtue (or lack thereof) of the notion. I accord the CI above-average credibility for a Third Tier source (2.5, as some have put it), because of the high likelihood that it entails the direct input of Anno himself.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sat Dec 15, 2007 2:13 pm a lot of people don't want the secrets revealed.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 15, 2007 5:55 pm UrsusArctos wrote: En Taro Reichu! *Bows*

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sat Dec 15, 2007 9:09 pm We don't know if Artanis ever used it in conjunction with Tassadar "En Taro Tassadar!" when Tassadar was still alive. But, if you think it ain't appropriate... i've fixed it.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Dec 15, 2007 9:30 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: a lot of people don't want the secrets revealed.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Dec 16, 2007 3:03 am Remember that you have to be consistent and reject sub-primary canon on a universal basis. ANYthing not in the show = bad. (So, for example, if I ever see you use the name "Tabris", I'll make you take a switch to your wrists.) This personal hangup will have to be put aside for community work, though, as mentioned. A standardized policy on canon management is mandatory , and the Tiers continue to serve their purpose.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Dec 16, 2007 5:26 am Don't forget to give him hell about the FAR.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Dec 16, 2007 6:31 am The FAR are henceforth "someone -- but not us". We'll call them Moon People, since they have no official name and all we know is that they build "Moons". Accordingly, their language is Moonspeak. The Seed of Life concept is also, accordingly, reduced back to plain ol' "source of life". Adam and Lilith are both things that come in "Moons" and crank out true and false successors for reasons we can only speculate upon. Man, the pre-CI days were so boring. But, on the plus side, we have far fewer "gods" to worry about and the Spear can be a stupid, will-less pitchfork if one so interprets it.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Dec 16, 2007 7:59 am That is so WIN.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Dec 16, 2007 8:06 pm Reichu wrote: Remember that you have to be consistent and reject sub-primary canon on a universal basis. ANYthing not in the show = bad.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Dec 16, 2007 8:51 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: That is so WIN.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Mon Dec 17, 2007 7:56 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Anonymous_Evafan wrote: a lot of people don't want the secrets revealed.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Mon Dec 17, 2007 8:20 pm *Treebeard voice* Hmmmmmmmmmmmmmmm! V, Tolkien kept changing his ideas and his worldview as he progressed. When Lord of the Rings was written, Gandalf wasn't just hinted at being something other than human- he was almost explicitly stated as being some sort of divinity. Doesn't he tell Gimli, "And I am dangerous, more dangerous than any of you." ? Gandalf was divine, but I'm not sure if Tolkien had completely settled his concepts of the Valar and the Maiar and the situation that had led to Gandalf going to Middle-Earth in the first place. To reveal the Valar and the Maiar so early on would have been distracting, to say the least, and would've added to confusion and brought up a whole lot more questions than answers. So far, you might say that we have a few Gandalfs in NGE, but be glad there are no Tom Bombadils. About the Rebuild continuum...Reichu, check your PMs...I've got something to ask you.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Tue Dec 18, 2007 8:16 pm Well what I'm saying is, to use the Tolkien example, there are 1 of 2 things: "Gandalf" type things: Things which are not obviously apparent within the narrative because the storyteller did not walk us by the hand through a heap of exposition, instead using a minimalist approach. Nonetheless, the answers to exist, they're just not readily apparent. "Tomb Bombadil" type things: things which are MEANT to represent "the unknown", specifically. Tolkien refused to elaborate on who or what Tom Bombadil was exactly, citing that essentially, he represents the unknown stuff that's always just around the corner that we can never fully know. Tolkien did hint that he had actually come up with a personal explanation of who he was, but he made it a point that the reading public should never know and he was supposed to represent "the unknown". My point is that within the series, the Angels are not "Tom Bombadil" type things, but "Gandalf" type things The Angels are not meant to represent "the Unknown, which we can never comprehend" The "Primary Theme" of the Angels is they are a different, alien form of life (so different they transcend our previously held conceptions of "life")...locked in direct evolutionary conflict with Mankind. It's about survival of the species and evolutionary conflict. A secondary theme is, yes, that human science cannot fully explain how the Angels function. --->This doesn't mean we know absolutely nothing about the Angels. Rather than saying of the Angels simply "their origin and purpose is a total mystery, they represent whatever you want about the Unknown"....what CAN be said is "we understand that they are an alternate never-before conceptualized form of life, who we are locked in an evolutionary struggle with"..... there might be an accusation that this "shows the man behind the curtain" (Wizard of Oz) but I don't think that occurs: we know what the "origin" and "purpose" of the Angels is, but WHAT they are remains an enigma. Physically, they baffle human science. Human science has been defeated in its attempts to understand the Angels and Man has truly reached the limits of what he can "know" some might say.....but this doesn't stop Seele and Nerv from Tinkering With Things They Don't Fully Understand. There's a thematic element from "Children of Men" (the BOOK) that human science cannot explain the infertility crisis or why it's happening, and this in large part has contributed to mankind's despair: if they knew "it's a virus, a virus we call (blank)" even if they can't cure it, they'd feel better. BUt they have NO IDEA what's causing it: Virus? Bacteria? Pollution? EM Interference? Act of God? Ozone depletion? And thus lack of knowledge has led to their despair; science was defeated. Similarly, we know more or less where the Angels come from and what they're trying to do (just as we know that "the infertility crisis has resulted in no more children") but their nature baffles human science and understanding ("we have no idea, however, what is causing the infertility crisis, be it virus or pollution") Thus, the "Mystique" of the Angels is not damaged by actually knowing "they're from space, created by the First Ancestral Race, just like humans were"....as we really still don't understand how they function. Nonetheless, Anno clearly (From not only CI but Proposal) intended for the FAR explanation for the Angels from the beginning of the series.--->They are thus a "Gandalf" type thing, not a "Tom Bombadil" type thing. Yes, there is a "Tom Bombadil" type thing in the story; The First Ancestral Race themselves (the Monolith Dudes as I call them, as they're like the First Ones from Clarke's 2001) As for "going beyond the walls of the series" that doesn't happen at all: they do hint that Gandalf is not a man but an immortal, and they DO hint that the FAR played a role in the creation of the Angels within the narrative of the series: "the White Moon and Black Moon are clearly artificial and clearly identical. An intelligence other than Man created them (Gendo)" "humans come from Lilith, Angels from Adam (Misato)". There is thus a difference between "they went beyond the walls of the narrative" and "they used a minimalist approach and didn't haphazardly dump exposition on us".

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Dec 18, 2007 10:14 pm wait im lost, why is Misato Adam?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Dec 18, 2007 10:46 pm Ascribing a quote with confusing notation.
